elements.sent-2411-238: Before using the PDV for any prediction task, we canonically derived the cumulative distribution function for each individual descriptor from the training set and used it to normalise the associated descriptor-values.
elements.sent-2411-239: The final PDV $\mathcal{F}$ was thus always contained in the hypercube $[0,1]^{200}$.
elements.sent-2411-240: This normalisation-step prevents certain machine-learning algorithms such as multilayer perceptrons from putting disproportionate attention on large-range features while ignoring small-range features.
elements.sent-2411-241: Below we give a list of advantages $\bm{(+)}$ and disadvantages $\bm{(-)}$ of PDVs as a molecular featurisation method.
elements.sent-2411-243: PDVs can be computed quickly, even for large molecular data sets.
elements.sent-2411-245: Certain descriptors quantify straightforward physicochemical properties of a compound such as its molecular weight or its heavy atom count.
elements.sent-2411-246: Such descriptors can be immediately understood and interpreted by chemical experts.
elements.sent-2411-247: However, it is important to note that there also exists a large number of potentially useful descriptors whose chemical interpretation is not obvious at all, such as the Balaban index discussed in Example~\ref{ex: balaban_index .}
elements.sent-2411-249: PDVs are easy to use and can be automatically generated without sophisticated technical knowledge via publicly available cheminformatics libraries such as the Python-package RDKit~\citep{landrum2006rdkit .}
elements.sent-2411-250: Chemically reasonable initial embedding of chemical space.
elements.sent-2411-251: PDVs tend to automatically calculate basic physicochemical features of molecules and thus immediately provide an a priori embedding of chemical space that is useful across a wide range of cheminformatics tasks.
elements.sent-2411-252: In particular, this means that basic chemical features that are relevant across many applications do not need to be inferred statistically from a molecular representation from scratch every time a model is trained on a new data set.
elements.sent-2411-253: This can be seen in contrast to trainable methods like GNNs which continuously need to relearn a reasonable embedding of chemical space at every new training cycle (unless they have been combined with suitable self-supervised or transfer learning approaches).
elements.sent-2411-254: PDVs with a dimensionality $l \leq 200$ often already provide useful feature vectors for molecular machine learning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-255: This can be seen in contrast to extended-connectivity fingerprints (ECFPs) which we will introduce in Section~\ref{sec: ecfps and which normally require a length of at least $l \geq 1024$ to reach acceptable performance.}
elements.sent-2411-256: The relatively low dimensionality of PDVs can decrease the risk of costly downstream-computations and overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-258: While ECFPs and message-passing GNNs, which we will both introduce in Section~\ref{sec: ecfps and Section~\ref{sec: gnns , are only able to extract features from local circular substructures, PDVs can directly express global high-level properties of a molecule such as its predicted $\log(P)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: PDVs are generally based on fixed algorithms that cannot be trained in a differentiable manner to learn features from more explicit molecular representations.
elements.sent-2411-260: In this particular sense we refer to PDVs as non-trainable.
elements.sent-2411-261: It is worth noting though that computed PDVs can still be adapted to a given data set to some extent via the application of additional downstream methods such as data-dependent feature selection and normalisation procedures (like the removal of low-variance or correlated descriptors).
elements.sent-2411-262: Strictly speaking such common procedures can certainly also be considered forms of training (i.e.~forms or learning from input data).
elements.sent-2411-263: The non-differentiability of PDVs might cause an information bottleneck through which important chemical information cannot pass.
elements.sent-2411-265: Since PDVs are not differentiable and normally do not contain tunable hyperparameters, one of the few ways to adapt them to a given prediction task is via the choice of physicochemical-descriptor functions that compose the final PDV.
elements.sent-2411-266: However, the optimal descriptor-choice is almost never obvious and usually requires the application of expert-knowledge or technical feature selection algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-268: Trainable molecular featurisation methods like message-passing GNNs are parametric families of functions that can represent an infinite number of potential feature mappings into a continuous real vector space.
elements.sent-2411-269: The composition of a PDV on the other hand is restricted to a finite number of a few thousand human-engineered non-trainable non-tunable physicochemical-descriptor functions.
elements.sent-2411-270: As a result, PDVs can only represent a finite number of distinct feature mappings defined by the initial descriptor-choices.
elements.sent-2411-271: This finite family of feature mappings might not always be sufficient to describe all of the relevant information for a chemical prediction task; a suitable descriptor-function for the problem might simply not be available.
elements.subsection-2411-003: A Short Background on Structural Fingerprints
elements.sent-2411-272: In this section, we will discuss the extended-connectivity fingerprint~(ECFP)~\citep{morgan1965generation,rogers2010extended , which is part of a larger family of molecular featurisation methods called structural fingerprints.}
elements.sent-2411-273: The essential idea behind structural fingerprints is to algorithmically detect and encode features that express parts of the chemical structure of a molecule.
elements.sent-2411-274: Often, structural fingerprints can be represented as bit vectors that express the presence or absence of certain chemical substructures within the input compound.
elements.sent-2411-275: Well-known structural fingerprints include the $166$-bit MACCS fingerprint~\citep{durant2002reoptimization and the $881$-bit PubChem fingerprint~\citep{pubchemfp2009,han2008developing .}}
elements.sent-2411-276: Both of these examples belong to a family of dictionary-based fingerprints that check the presence or absence of substructures from a predefined dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-277: The dictionaries underlying MACCS and PubChem fingerprints include $166$ and $881$ unique substructures respectively, which explains the dimensionalities of these featurisations.
elements.sent-2411-278: Another important family of structural fingerprints is given by enumeration-based fingerprints.
elements.sent-2411-279: These fingerprints exhaustively enumerate all substructures of a molecule that can be detected via a certain search strategy.
elements.sent-2411-280: An example are Daylight fingerprints~\citep{daylightfp which enumerate all substructures within an input compound that correspond to groups of atoms and bonds that form paths up to a chosen length.}
elements.sent-2411-281: As we will see in this section, ECFPs too fall into the category of enumeration-based fingerprints since they enumerate all circular substructures in a molecule up to a chosen radius.
elements.sent-2411-282: Because enumeration-based fingerprints are not constrained by a fixed-sized dictionary of substructures, they can often detect an extremely large number of distinct fragments.
elements.sent-2411-283: It is thus not immediately obvious how the substructures found within a particular compound should be transformed into a reasonably-sized bit-vector.
elements.sent-2411-284: The standard technique to address this issue is by using a hash function to fold the detected fragments into a bit-vector representation of feasible dimensionality and accept the fact that colliding bits might lead to a certain level of ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-285: In Chapter~\ref{chap: ecfps_sort_and_slice and in Section~\ref{sec: diff_substruc_pool , we will explore some interesting alternatives to this hashing procedure for the vectorisation of structural fingerprints.}}
elements.sent-2411-286: In our work, we focus on ECFPs out of all available structural fingerprints.
elements.sent-2411-287: Our reasons for this include that ECFPs have been used widely in the area of cheminformatics and molecular machine-learning and are well-known to perform well on a diverse set of tasks~\citep{riniker2013open,duvenaud2015convolutional,webel2020revealing,rogers2005using,alvarsson2014ligand,gilmer2017neural .}
elements.sent-2411-288: Moreover, as we will discover in Section~\ref{sec: gnns , ECFPs also exhibit some striking similarities with modern message-passing-GNN architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-289: In some sense, ECFPs can be interpreted as a non-differentiable version of message-passing GNNs, which makes a comparison between these two approaches interesting.
elements.sent-2411-290: We will use the rest of this section to give a mathematical description of ECFPs along with a critical discussion of their strengths and weaknesses.
elements.sent-2411-291: The ECFP algorithm is a molecular featurisation method that maps a SMILES string (or its associated molecular graph) to a structural fingerprint that can be represented as a bit vector.
elements.sent-2411-292: The fundamental ideas underlying ECFPs were originally introduced by~\citet{morgan1965generation in $1965$; however, modern implementations of the ECFP are largely based on a detailed technical description given by~\citet{rogers2010extended in $2010$.}}
elements.sent-2411-293: ECFPs provide a simple yet powerful technique to encode information about the chemical structure of an input compound.
elements.sent-2411-294: The algorithm is dependent on two predefined hyperparameters: the desired fingerprint length $l \in \mathbb{N}$ and the maximum radius $R \in \mathbb{N _{0}$ of the receptive field.}
elements.sent-2411-295: An ECFP of length $l$ takes the form of a binary vector \begin{equation}F = (f_1,...,f_l) \in \{0,1\}^{l}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-296: Up to a certain level of ambiguity due to bit collisions which we will discuss below, each component $f_i$ in $\mathcal{F}$ is associated with the presence or absence of a particular circular subgraph, equipped with specific atom and bond features inherited from the input molecule, centered around a given atom.
elements.sent-2411-297: Denote with \begin{equation}N(a) \coloneqq \{\tilde{a} \in A \ \vert \ \{ \tilde{a}, a \} \in B \}\end{equation} the set of neighbouring atoms of $a$ and denote with \begin{equation}M(a) \coloneqq \{\{a_1, a_2\} \in B \ \vert \ a \in \{a_1, a_2\} \}\end{equation} the set of bonds that are attached to $a$.
elements.sent-2411-298: Now let $(A^a_r, B^a_r)$ be a sequence of subgraphs of $(A,B)$ for $r \in \mathbb{N _{0}$ constructed via the following iterative scheme: \begin{equation}(A^a_0, B^a_0) \coloneqq (\{a\}, \{\}), [8pt] A^a_r = A^a_{r-1} \cup \bigcup_{\tilde{a} \in A^a_{r-1}} N(\tilde{a}), \quad B^a_r = B^a_{r-1} \cup \bigcup_{\tilde{a} \in A^a_{r-1}} M(\tilde{a}).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}(A^a_0, B^a_0) \coloneqq (\{a\}, \{\}), [8pt] A^a_r = A^a_{r-1} \cup \bigcup_{\tilde{a} \in A^a_{r-1}} N(\tilde{a}), \quad B^a_r = B^a_{r-1} \cup \bigcup_{\tilde{a} \in A^a_{r-1}} M(\tilde{a}).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-299: Then $(A^a_r, B^a_r)$ is called the circular subgraph of $\mathcal{G}$ with center atom $a$ and radius $r$.
elements.sent-2411-300: If $\mathcal{G}$ is equipped with atom and bond feature vectors, then these are inherited by $(A^a_r, B^a_r)$.
elements.sent-2411-301: Circular subgraphs with varying radii for an example compound are depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:circular_subgraphs_example .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width = 0.8 \linewidth]{figures/circular_subgraphs_example.pdf} \caption[Circular chemical subgraphs of varying radii.]{Circular subgraphs of varying radii for a central nitrogen atom in an example molecule.} \label{fig:circular_subgraphs_example}
elements.sent-2411-302: The fingerprint hyperparameter $R$ defines the maximum radius of any circular subgraph whose presence or absence is indicated in the fingerprint $\mathcal{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-303: Circular subgraphs that are structurally isomorphic are further distinguished according to their inherited atom and bond features, i.e.~two structurally isomorphic circular subgraphs with distinct atom or bond features correspond to different components of $\mathcal{F}$.
elements.sent-2411-304: For chemical bonds, this distinction is made on the basis of simple bond types: single, double, triple, or aromatic.
elements.sent-2411-305: To distinguish atoms, ECFPs usually either use standard or pharmacophoric atom features and we discuss both variants below.
elements.sent-2411-306: There also exists a non-binary version of the ECFP in which each component of $\mathcal{F}$ does not simply indicate the presence or absence of a circular subgraph, but the exact number of occurrences of the subgraph within the input compound.
elements.sent-2411-307: This version is sometimes referred to as ECFP with counts as opposed to the binary version mentioned above which is also called ECFP without counts.
elements.sent-2411-308: Throughout this thesis, unless specifically stated otherwise, we focus on ECFPs without counts, as they are used significantly more frequently in practice and allow for an easier algorithmic description.
elements.sent-2411-309: However, it is straightforward to extend the methods described in this section to ECFPs with counts.
elements.sent-2411-310: We now give a technical description of the algorithm used for the generation of ECFPs as specified in the article of~\citet{rogers2010extended .}
elements.sent-2411-311: A molecular compound $\mathcal{M}$, usually represented via a SMILES string $\mathcal{S}$.
elements.sent-2411-312: Note that $\mathcal{S}$ contains the structural information of the molecular graph $\mathcal{G = (A,B)$.}
elements.sent-2411-313: A function $f$ specifying the atom feature vectors for $A$ using either standard or pharmacophoric features.
elements.sent-2411-314: A function $g$ specifying the bond types for $B$ using encodings for labels in the set $\{\text{single, double, triple, aromatic}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-315: A fingerprint length $l \in \mathbb{N}$, often chosen to be in $\{1024, 2048\}$.
elements.sent-2411-316: A fingerprint radius $R \in \mathbb{N _{0}$, often chosen to be in $\{1, 2, 3\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-317: Initialisation: A deterministic hash function $h$ is chosen which maps integer vectors of arbitrary lengths into a large index set such as \begin{equation}\{1,...,2^{32}\}\end{equation} in a pseudo-random and uniformly distributed manner.
elements.sent-2411-318: The map $h$ is then used to hash each atom feature vector $f(a)$ to a single integer $ h(f(a)) \in \{1,...,2^{32}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-319: The integers in the set \begin{equation}I_{0} \coloneqq \{ h(f(a)) \ \vert \ a \in A\} \subseteq \{1,...,2^{32}\}\end{equation} are called initial atom identifiers.
elements.sent-2411-320: Iterative phase for substructure-enumeration: The initialisation step is followed by $R$ iterative steps, whereby at each step all atom identifiers are updated and saved.
elements.sent-2411-321: At each step $r \in \{1,..., R\}$, each atom $a \in A$ first starts off equipped with its respective atom identifier in $I_{r-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-322: The new integer atom identifier for $a$ is generated as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-323: A vector of integers $J_{a,r}$ is created that contains the integer $r$ in the first position and the current atom identifier of $a$ in the second position.
elements.sent-2411-324: All neighbouring atoms that have a bond to $a$ are ordered in lexicographic order according to their bond types (single $= 1$, double $ = 2$, triple $ = 3$, aromatic $ = 4$) and then according to their current integer atom identifiers.
elements.sent-2411-325: A loop is performed over all neighbouring atoms according to this established order; for each neighbouring atom, first the bond type and then the current atom identifier is appended to the end of the integer vector $J_{a,r}$.
elements.sent-2411-326: The integer $h(J_{a,r ) \in \{1,...,2^{32}\}$ is computed and interpreted as the new atom identifier for $a$.}
elements.sent-2411-327: After the set of new atom identifiers \begin{equation}I_{r} \coloneqq \{ h(J_{a,r}) \ \vert \ a \in A \} \subseteq \{1,...,2^{32}\}\end{equation} has been computed, the current atom identifiers are simultaneously updated to correspond to the elements in $I_{r}$.
elements.sent-2411-328: Note that by construction each atom identifier in $I_{r}$ represents an integer label that can be mapped to a particular circular subgraph with radius $r$ and associated atom and bond features.
elements.sent-2411-329: Thus, if two circular subgraphs receive the same integer label they can be assumed to be isomorphic (ignoring rare ambiguities caused by possible hash collision that could lead to two distinct circular subgraphs having the same integer label).
elements.sent-2411-330: After the completion of $R$ iterations, the atom identifiers from all iterative stages are collected in one set: \begin{equation}I \coloneqq \bigcup_{r = 0}^{R} I_r \subseteq \{1, ..., 2^{32}\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-331: To eliminate this redundancy, all but one of the structural-duplicate-identifiers are systematically removed from $\mathcal{I}$ using a method based on sets of bond features.
elements.sent-2411-332: For sake of brevity, further details of this technical removal process are omitted from this description, but full details can be found in the article from~\citet{rogers2010extended .\newline Creation of hashed vectorial fingerprint: After the elimination of all structural duplicates in $\mathcal{I}$, yet another (arbitrary) standard hash function \begin{equation}\tilde{h} : \{1, ..., 2^{32}\} \to \{1, ..., l\}\end{equation} is chosen to map the integers in $\mathcal{I}$ into the much smaller set \begin{equation}\{1, ..., l\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-333: This folding procedure creates a fingerprint-set \begin{equation}F \coloneqq \{\tilde{h}(i) \ \vert \ i \in I \} \subseteq \{1, ..., l\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-334: Finally, $F$ is transformed into a binary fingerprint-vector \begin{equation}F \coloneqq (f_1, ..., f_l) \in \{0,1\}^l\end{equation} by setting \begin{equation}f_i \coloneqq \left\{ {ll} 1 \quad i \in F, 0 \quad i \notin F,  \right. \quad \quad \forall i \in \{1,...,l\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-335: Note that the larger the fingerprint dimension $l$ gets compared to the number of detected substructures (as controlled by $R$), the more likely it becomes that there are no bit collisions.
elements.sent-2411-336: In other words, the more likely it becomes that each dimensional component $f_i$ of $\mathcal{F}$ informs about the presence or absence of one particular atom identifier in $\mathcal{I}$ and thus about the presence or absence of one unambiguous circular subgraph with atom and bond features within the input compound (ignoring rare ambiguities where two distinct circular subgraphs end up with the same atom identifier due to hash collisions).
elements.sent-2411-337: The bit $f_i$ is then set to $1$ if and only if the circular substructure is present anywhere in the molecule, otherwise $f_i$ is set to~$0$.
elements.sent-2411-338: However, if $l$ becomes small relative to the number of detected substructures then more and more hash collisions start to occur, which degrades the quality of the fingerprint.
elements.sent-2411-339: Such hash collisions cause a fingerprint-component $f_i$ to become ambiguous and correspond to one out of several possible atom identifiers and thus to distinct circular substructures.
elements.sent-2411-340: Therefore, $l$ must be chosen sufficiently large as to guarantee the expressivity of the ECFP.
elements.sent-2411-341: In the literature, ECFP featurisations with radius $R$ are often written in the form ECFP$2R$ with $2R$ being the fingerprint diameter.
elements.sent-2411-342: For example, the frequently used $1024$-bit ECFP$4$-featurisation describes an ECFP with radius $R = 2$ and length $l = 1024$.
elements.sent-2411-343: In our work, we used the Python cheminformatics-library RDKit~\citep{landrum2006rdkit to generate ECFPs from SMILES strings.}
elements.sent-2411-344: To distinguish atoms, ECFPs as implemented in RDKit~\citep{landrum2006rdkit use six standard features.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Optionally, the algorithm also allows for the stereochemical distinction between atoms with respect to tetrahedral R-S chirality.
elements.sent-2411-346: There also exist alternative binary atom features that were designed to be more reflective of the abstract function that an atom might play in pharmacological chemistry.
elements.sent-2411-347: When these pharmacophoric atom features~\citep{rogers2010extended are used instead of the standard features, then one speaks of functional-connectivity fingerprints (FCFPs).}
elements.sent-2411-348: The standard and pharmacophoric atom features for the ECFP algorithm are listed in Table~\ref{tab: ecfp_atom_features .}
elements.figure-2411-005: {\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3} \begin{tabular}{V{3} p{7cm}| p{7cm} V{3}} \hlineB{3} \multicolumn{1}{V{3} c|}{Standard Atom Features} \multicolumn{1}{c V{3}}{Pharmacophoric Atom Features} Atomic number Hydrogen-bond acceptor (yes/no) Total degree Hydrogen-bond donor (yes/no) Number of hydrogen neighbours Negatively ionisable (yes/no) Formal charge Positively ionisable (yes/no) Isotope Aromatic (yes/no) Part of a ring (yes/no) Halogen (yes/no) Optional: tetrahedral R-S chirality Optional: tetrahedral R-S chirality \hlineB{3} \end{tabular}} \caption[Atom features for ECFPs.]{Standard and pharmacophoric atom features used for the two versions of the ECFP algorithm.} \label{tab: ecfp_atom_features}
elements.sent-2411-349: As can be seen, there is an overlap between the standard atom features for ECFPs and the atom features in our molecular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-350: In certain molecular machine learning applications, replacing standard with pharmacophoric atom features might lead to increased performance and decreased learning time since important high-level atomic properties are presented to the learning model from the start and do not need to be inferred statistically.
elements.sent-2411-351: However, standard atom features contain more detailed information that could still be relevant for the prediction task and thus be leveraged by the learning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-352: Below we give a list of advantages $\bm{(+)}$ and disadvantages $\bm{(-)}$ of the ECFP algorithm as a molecular featurisation method.
elements.sent-2411-353: ECFPs can be computed rapidly, even for large molecular data sets.
elements.sent-2411-354: Since ECFPs contain information about the presence or absence of concrete chemical substructures (up to hash collisions) they can often be understood and interpreted in a straightforward manner.
elements.sent-2411-355: ECFPs are easy to use and can be automatically generated without sophisticated technical knowledge via publicly available cheminformatics libraries such as the Python-package RDKit~\citep{landrum2006rdkit .}
elements.sent-2411-356: ECFPs automatically express basic structural features of molecules and thus immediately provide an a priori embedding of chemical space that is useful across a wide range of cheminformatics tasks.
elements.sent-2411-357: In particular, this implies that basic structural features that are important across many applications do not need to be relearned from a molecular representation from scratch every time a model is trained on a new data set.
elements.sent-2411-359: ECFPs do not simply check the existence of substructures from a predefined finite list of chemical substructures (like dictionary-based structural fingerprints do), but are able to distinguish between an essentially infinite number of chemical subgraphs, albeit only circular ones.
elements.sent-2411-360: After its hyperparameters have been chosen, the ECFP transformation becomes a fixed method that produces the same task-agnostic features for a given compound across data sets.
elements.sent-2411-361: In particular, ECFPs cannot be trained in a differentiable manner to learn features from more explicit molecular representations.
elements.sent-2411-362: In this particular sense we refer to ECFPs as non-trainable.
elements.sent-2411-363: It should be noted though that computed ECFPs can nevertheless be adapted to a given data set to some degree via the application of additional downstream methods such as data-dependent feature selection and normalisation procedures; strictly speaking such procedures can certainly also be considered forms of training (i.e.~forms or learning from input data) and we will explore some of these techniques in Chapter~\ref{chap: ecfps_sort_and_slice .}
elements.sent-2411-364: The non-differentiability of ECFPs might cause an information bottleneck through which important chemical information cannot pass.
elements.sent-2411-365: Trade-off between dimensionality and hash collisions.
elements.sent-2411-366: Decreasing the fingerprint dimension~$l$ relative to the number of detected substructures increases the number of hash collisions.
elements.sent-2411-367: These collisions cause a loss of information and interpretability due to an increasing inability of the fingerprint to distinguish between non-identical circular substructures.
elements.sent-2411-368: Thus, lengths of $l \geq 1024$ are often a necessity to reach an acceptable performance.
elements.sent-2411-369: In a machine learning setting, this high dimensionality can lead to costly downstream-computations and increased risk of overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-371: ECFPs are based on a local neighbourhood-aggregation scheme for atoms in a molecule.
elements.sent-2411-372: By design, they are thus only capable of indicating the existence of local circular subgraphs.
elements.sent-2411-373: In particular, they cannot directly express global properties of a molecule.
elements.sent-2411-374: In $2015$, \citet{duvenaud2015convolutional published an influential article where they proposed an adaptive counterpart to ECFPs.}
elements.sent-2411-375: Their goal was to overcome some of the inherent limitations of the ECFP featurisation such as non-differentiability and high dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-376: Their work resulted in a trainable GNN architecture that could directly process molecular graphs of varying sizes as input; they reported competitive performance of their method relative to classical ECFPs at several canonical molecular prediction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-377: After this initial step, a multitude of other promising GNN architectures appeared rapidly within the molecular machine-learning community~\citep{kipf2016semi, kearnes2016molecular, hu2019strategies,yang2019analyzing, wu2020comprehensive, wieder2020compact, li2015gated,battaglia2016interaction,defferrard2016convolutional,liu2019chemi .}
elements.sent-2411-378: In a seminal article from $2017$,~\citet{gilmer2017neural managed to subsume almost all modern GNN architectures under an overarching mathematical framework which was further generalised and brought into its modern form by~\citet{bronstein2017geometric,bronstein2021geometric .}}
elements.sent-2411-379: Below we give a brief technical description of this framework known as message-passing.
elements.sent-2411-380: A chemical compound $\mathcal{M}$, represented via its molecular graph $\mathcal{G = (A,B)$.}
elements.sent-2411-381: An function $f$ specifying the atom feature vectors for $A$.
elements.sent-2411-382: A function $g$ specifying the bond feature vectors for $B$.
elements.sent-2411-383: A fingerprint length $l \in \mathbb{N}$, often chosen to be in $\{50,...,500\}$.
elements.sent-2411-384: A fingerprint radius $R \in \mathbb{N _{0}$, often chosen to be in $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-385: Message-passing phase: The first part of the algorithm consists of a message-passing phase in which the atom feature vectors of $\mathcal{G}$ are iteratively updated over $r \in \{1,...,R\}$ steps via a local neighbourhood-aggregation scheme.
elements.sent-2411-386: We denote the set of neighbours of an atom $a \in A$ as \begin{equation}N(a)\coloneqq \{\tilde{a} \in A: \{a, \tilde{a}\} \in B\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-387: At each step $r$, every atom $a \in A$ updates its associated feature vector from $f_{r-1 (a)$ to $f_{r (a)$ according to the following recursive relations: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \label{eq: mpnn_equations} f_{0}(a) \coloneqq f(a), [12pt] m_{r}(a) = \bigoplus \ \left\{w_r(f_{r-1}(a), f_{r-1}(\tilde{a}), g(\{a, \tilde{a}\})) \ \vert \ \tilde{a} \in N(a) \right\}_{\text{mul}}, [12pt] f_{r}(a) = u_r(f_{r-1}(a), m_{r}(a)). [4.5pt] \end{gathered}\end{equation} The vector-valued functions $(w_r)_{r = 1 ^{R}$ are called message-passing functions and the vector-valued functions $(u_r)_{r = 1 ^{R}$ are called atom-updating functions; both types of maps frequently contain trainable neural networks.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \label{eq: mpnn_equations} f_{0}(a) \coloneqq f(a), [12pt] m_{r}(a) = \bigoplus \ \left\{w_r(f_{r-1}(a), f_{r-1}(\tilde{a}), g(\{a, \tilde{a}\})) \ \vert \ \tilde{a} \in N(a) \right\}_{mul}, [12pt] f_{r}(a) = u_r(f_{r-1}(a), m_{r}(a)). [4.5pt] \end{gathered}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-388: The vector $m_{r (a)$ can be interpreted as an aggregated message that the atom $a$ receives at step $r$ from neighbouring atoms along its associated chemical bonds to update its current feature vector.}
elements.sent-2411-389: Multisets, i.e.~sets that can contain multiple instances of the same element, denoted via $\{\}_{\text{mul }$ instead of $\{\}$, are required in the above recursion.}
elements.sent-2411-390: This is because two atom neighbours $\tilde{a _1, \tilde{a _2$ of $a$ could in theory produce identical messages \begin{equation}w_r(f_{r-1}(a), f_{r-1}(\tilde{a}_1), g(\{a, \tilde{a}_1\})) = w_r(f_{r-1}(a), f_{r-1}(\tilde{a}_2), g(\{a, \tilde{a}_2\})),\end{equation} but two such messages should still be counted as separate elements.}}
elements.sent-2411-391: The $\bm{\oplus}$-symbol represents a placeholder for a vector-valued and permutation-invariant multiset-function.
elements.sent-2411-392: Note that permutation-invariance is a basic requirement that each multiset functions must possess in order to be a well-defined function in the first place.
elements.sent-2411-393: The permutation-invariance is necessary to guarantee that the aggregated message $m_{r (a)$ is invariant under any (arbitrary) ordering imposed on the neighbours of~$a$.}
elements.sent-2411-394: Possible operators for $\bm{\oplus}$ include summation, averaging, or the componentwise computation of maxima.
elements.sent-2411-395: In most cases, however, the sum-operator is selected, \begin{equation}\label{eq: sum_op_pool} \bigoplus \coloneqq \sum,\end{equation} and we assume this choice by default unless stated otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-396: The multiset of atom feature vectors at a particular step $r \in \{0,...,R\}$, \begin{equation}f_r(A)_{mul} \coloneqq \{f_r(a) \ \vert \ a \in A\}_{mul},\end{equation} is often imagined to be located at the $r$-th layer of a multilayer graph with $R + 1$ layers.
elements.sent-2411-397: Note that the multiset of bond feature vectors \begin{equation}g(B)_{mul} \coloneqq \{ g(\{a,\tilde{a}\}) \ \vert \ \{a, \tilde{a}\} \in B \}_{mul}\end{equation} usually does not get updated, although this rule was successfully broken by~\citet{kearnes2016molecular .}
elements.sent-2411-398: In the most general case, this pooling operation is accomplished via the application of a sequence of vector-valued multiset-functions $(\tilde{\bm{\oplus _r)_{r=0 ^{R}$ along the sequence of graph layers.}}}
elements.sent-2411-399: The layerwise outputs can then be combined via another vector-valued function $q$ to a final graph-level fingerprint $\mathcal{F}$ of length~$l$.
elements.sent-2411-400: In mathematical terms the global pooling operation thus takes the following general form: \begin{equation}\label{eq: mpnn_pooling} q(\tilde{\bm{\oplus}}_0 f_0(A)_{\text{mul}},...,\tilde{\bm{\oplus}}_R f_R(A)_{\text{mul}}) \eqqcolon \mathcal{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{l}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-032: \begin{equation}\label{eq: mpnn_pooling} q(\tilde{\oplus}_0 f_0(A)_{mul},...,\tilde{\oplus}_R f_R(A)_{mul}) \eqqcolon F \in R^{l}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-401: Similar to before, the pooling maps $(\tilde{\bm{\oplus _r)_{r=0 ^{R}$ are chosen to be permutation-invariant multiset functions as to guarantee their invariance under any (arbitrary) atom ordering imposed on the input graph $\mathcal{G}$ and to guarantee that multiple instances of identical atom feature vectors are treated as separate elements.}}}
elements.sent-2411-402: Often each $\tilde{\bm{\oplus _r$ simply represents a summation or averaging operator, or the componentwise computation of maxima, although more powerful and expressive pooling maps exist.}}
elements.sent-2411-403: An overview of the total molecular-featurisation process via message-passing GNNs is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:mpgnn_overview .. \end{mdframed.}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width = 0.98 \linewidth]{figures/mpgnn_overview.pdf} \caption[Molecular featurisation via message-passing GNN.]{Schematic overview of the molecular-featurisation mechanism of a message-passing graph neural network~(GNN) with radius $R = 2$. All depicted functions may contain trainable deep-learning components.} \label{fig:mpgnn_overview}
elements.sent-2411-404: We can see that message-passing GNNs exhibit some striking similarities with ECFPs.
elements.sent-2411-405: The fingerprint radius $R$ of a GNN defines the maximum diameter of its circular receptive field and plays an analogous role to the hyperparameter $R$ of the same name for ECFPs.
elements.sent-2411-406: In both featurisation methods a local neighbourhood-aggregation scheme is applied to iteratively update the atom feature vectors of the molecular graph.
elements.sent-2411-407: Unlike ECFPs, however, message-passing GNNs can learn from graph-shaped input data in a differentiable manner: the functions $(w_r)_{r = 1 ^{R , (u_r)_{r = 1 ^{R}$ normally contain a trainable deep-learning component.}}}
elements.sent-2411-408: The graph-level feature vector $\mathcal{F}$ can be fed into a neural prediction head (such as a multilayer perceptron) which enables the resulting end-to-end architecture to train its weights on a supervised task via backpropagation and standard gradient-based optimisation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-409: We now describe an early GNN model that has been used frequently in the literature due to its relative simplicity and computational efficiency: the graph convolutional network~(GCN) introduced in $2016$ by~\citet{kipf2016semi .. Let.}
elements.sent-2411-410: Then the atom feature vector updates in a GCN are governed by the following dynamics: \begin{equation}\label{eq: gcn_matrix_recursion} F_{r} \coloneqq \text{ReLU}(\bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \bar{S} \bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}} F_{r-1} W_{r-1}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-411: This updating rule can be motivated by a first-order approximation of localised spectral filters on graphs.
elements.sent-2411-412: For details on this derivation, we refer the reader to the original article~\citep{kipf2016semi .}
elements.sent-2411-413: We now show that GCNs are indeed message-passing GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-414: Note that for each $a_i \in A$ the associated entry of the diagonal matrix $\bar{D}$ takes the form \begin{equation}\bar{D}_{i,i} = \sum_{j = 1}^n \bar{S}_{i,j} = \deg(a_i) + 1\end{equation} and we can thus write $\bar{D _{i,i \coloneqq \bar{D (a_i)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-415: Now let $a_i, a_j \in A$ be two neighbouring atoms in $\mathcal{G}$, i.e.~$a_j \in N(a_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-416: We choose \begin{equation}w_r(f_{r-1}(a_i), f_{r-1}(a_j), g(\{a_i, a_j\})) \coloneqq \bar{D}(a_i)^{-1/2} \bar{D}(a_j)^{-1/2} f_{r-1}(a_j)\end{equation} as our message-passing functions and \begin{equation}u_r(f_{r-1}(a_i), m_{r}(a_i)) \coloneqq ReLU(W^{T}_{r-1}(\bar{D}(a_i)^{-1} f_{r-1}(a_i) + m_r(a_i)))\end{equation} as our atom-updating functions.
elements.sent-2411-417: To guarantee that the functions $w_r$ and $u_r$ are indeed well-defined, we assume without loss of generality that the node degree information $\bar{D (a_i)$ is implicitly included in each initial atom feature vector $f_0(a_i)$ and then simply gets copied from $f_{r-1 (a_i)$ to $f_r(a_i)$ to assure its availability at each iterative feature update.}}
elements.sent-2411-418: The aggregated message for $a_i$ is now given by \begin{equation}m_{r}(a_i) = \sum \{ \bar{D}(a_i)^{-1/2} \bar{D}(a_j)^{-1/2} f_{r-1}(a_j) \ \vert \ a_j \in N(a_i) \}_{mul} = \sum_{j = 1}^n \bar{D}(a_i)^{-1/2} S_{i,j} \bar{D}(a_j)^{-1/2} f_{r-1}(a_j) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-419: As explained in~\ref{eq: sum_op_pool above, here the expression of the form $\sum \{...\}_{\text{mul }$ simply represents the sum of all elements in the multiset $\{...\}_{\text{mul }$, which means that the summation-operator is used as a permutation-invariant multiset-function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-420: If we denote the $i$-th row-vector of the matrix $\bar{D ^{-\frac{1 {2 \bar{S \bar{D ^{-\frac{1 {2 }$ with $[\bar{D ^{-\frac{1 {2 \bar{S \bar{D ^{-\frac{1 {2 ]_{[i,:]}$ then it follows that \begin{equation}f_r(a_i) = ReLU\big(W^{T}_{r-1}(\bar{D}(a_i)^{-1} f_{r-1}(a_i) + m_r(a_i))\big) = ReLU\bigg(W^{T}_{r-1} \sum_{j = 1}^n \bar{D}(a_i)^{-1/2} \bar{S}_{i,j} \bar{D}(a_j)^{-1/2} f_{r-1}(a_j) \bigg) = ReLU\bigg(W^{T}_{r-1} \big( [\bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \bar{S} \bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}]_{[i,:]} F_{r-1} \big)^{T} \bigg) = ReLU\bigg(W^{T}_{r-1} F_{r-1}^{T} [\bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \bar{S} \bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}]_{[i,:]}^{T} \bigg)\end{equation} We can now finally conclude that \begin{equation}f_r(a_i)^{T} = ReLU\bigg([\bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \bar{S} \bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}}]_{[i,:]} F_{r-1} W_{r-1} \bigg)\end{equation} and therefore \begin{equation}F_{r} = ReLU(\bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}} \bar{S} \bar{D}^{-\frac{1}{2}} F_{r-1} W_{r-1}).\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-421: We constructed our proof of Proposition~\ref{prop: gcns_are_mpgnns as a slightly adapted and more detailed version of the original proof we found in the article of~\citet{gilmer2017neural .}}
elements.sent-2411-422: Note that while GCNs are sensitive to the connectivity structure of their input graph, like many GNNs they do not take into account bond feature vectors.
elements.sent-2411-423: GCNs have been demonstrated to work reasonably well for a variety of graph-based prediction tasks~\citep{wang2021molclr, dwivedi2020benchmarking,tavakoli2020continuous,liu2020towards,hu2020open,kipf2016semi .}
elements.sent-2411-424: In 2018, \citet{xu2018powerful published an influential article in which they introduced the graph isomorphism network (GIN) model.}
elements.sent-2411-425: The GIN was developed to overcome some of the theoretical shortcomings of GCNs and other popular GNN architectures available at the time.
elements.sent-2411-426: We will discuss the highly relevant motivation of Xu et al.~and their theoretical insights in the next section, but will first give a brief description of their proposed model.
elements.sent-2411-427: The message-passing mechanism of GINs expressed via Equations~\ref{eq: mpnn_equations is defined in a straightforward manner via \begin{equation}w_r(f_{r-1}(a), f_{r-1}(\tilde{a}), g(\{a, \tilde{a}\})) = f_{r-1}(\tilde{a})\end{equation} and \begin{equation}u_r(f_{r-1}(a), m_{r}(a)) = \phi_{r}((1 + \epsilon_r) f_{r-1}(a) + m_r(a)).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-428: Here $\epsilon_r$ is a small (optionally trainable) parameter and $\phi_{r}$ is a trainable multilayer perceptron with at least one hidden layer.
elements.sent-2411-429: Note that $\phi_r$ is specifically required to be more powerful than the shallow neural network $x^{T \mapsto \text{ReLU (x^{T W)$ used in the definition of GCNs in Equation~\ref{eq: gcn_matrix_recursion , which has no hidden layer and therefore does not fulfill the requirements of the universal approximation theorem for feedforward neural networks~\citep{hornik1989multilayer .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-430: One can write the atom feature updating process for GINs in the compact recursive form \begin{equation}f_r(a) = \phi_{r}\Big((1 + \epsilon_r) f_{r-1}(a) + \sum \ f_{r-1}(N(a))_{mul} \Big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-431: Like GCNs, standard GINs also ignore bond feature vectors, although modifications of the GIN architecture that do consider bond features have been used successfully~\citep{hu2019strategies .}
elements.sent-2411-432: GINs are easy to implement, work well in practice and are currently reaching state-of-the-art performance in a variety of applications~\citep{kim2020understanding, wang2021molclr,dwivedi2020benchmarking, xu2018powerful .}
elements.subsection-2411-007: Theoretical Expressivity of Graph Neural Networks
elements.sent-2411-433: The relatively old GCNs are still popular GNN models.
elements.sent-2411-434: However, they come with a significant caveat.
elements.sent-2411-435: This caveat was pointed out by \citet{xu2018powerful in their seminal $2018$ paper and used as a motivation for the design of the GIN.}
elements.sent-2411-436: They proved that GCNs (as well as a plethora of other commonly-used GNN architectures) sometimes map non-isomorphic graphs to identical vectorial representations and thus suffer from a lack of theoretical expressivity.
elements.sent-2411-437: In other words, they discovered that GCNs and many other popular GNN models cannot learn to distinguish certain simple graph structures, to the point where in some instances they severely underfit the training set.
elements.sent-2411-438: The GIN model was specifically developed to overcome this problematic lack of expressivity.
elements.sent-2411-439: And indeed, \citet{xu2018powerful managed to prove that GINs are strictly more expressive than GCNs and a number of other popular GNN models.}
elements.sent-2411-440: They specifically demonstrated that GINs can distinguish certain non-isomorphic graphs that GCNs cannot.
elements.sent-2411-441: In addition they showed that GINs are as expressive as standard message-passing GNNs can ever be; GINs are in the subclass of maximally expressive message-passing GNNs and are exactly as powerful at distinguishing non-isomorphic graphs as the canonical $1$-Weisfeiler-Lehman~($1$-WL) graph isomorhism test~\citep{weisfeiler1968reduction .}
elements.sent-2411-442: We now iteratively construct a sequence of functions $(c_r)_{r \in \mathbb{N _{0 }$ on the set of graph nodes $A$.}}
elements.sent-2411-443: Each value $c_r(a)$ is imagined to be a momentary colouring of the node $a \in A$ at step $r$.
elements.sent-2411-444: The colouring functions are constructed via the following iterative scheme: \begin{equation}c_0(a) \coloneqq f(a), c_{r}(a) = h(c_{r-1}(a), c_{r-1}(N(a))_{mul}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-445: Here $a \in A$ is a node of $\mathcal{G}$ and $h$ is an injective hash function that maps each vertex colour along with its multiset of neighbouring colours $c_{r-1 (N(a))_{\text{mul }$ to a new unique colour.}}
elements.sent-2411-446: This procedure terminates after a finite number of $R$ steps when a stable colouring $c_R$ is reached that cannot be changed by subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-447: Let \begin{equation}c_R(A)_{mul} \coloneqq \{ c_R(a) \ \vert \ a \in A \}_{mul}\end{equation} be called the multiset of final vertex colours.
elements.sent-2411-448: If the multisets of final vertex colours of two input graphs $\mathcal{G _1, \mathcal{G _2$ are distinct, then the graphs are guaranteed not to be isomorphic.}}
elements.sent-2411-449: However, if the multisets of final vertex colours are identical, then the graphs are potentially but not necessarily isomorphic.
elements.sent-2411-450: If two non-isomorphic graphs reach identical multisets of final vertex colours, we say that the $1$-WL test cannot distinguish these graphs.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/wl_counterexample} \caption[Non-isomorphic graphs that cannot be distinguished by the $1$-WL test.]{Example of two non-isomorphic graphs that cannot be distinguished by the $1$-WL test if all nodes are assumed to have identical initial colourings. Image source:~\citep{bouritsas2022improving}.} \label{fig:wlcounterexample}
elements.sent-2411-451: However, in spite of the existence of such counterexamples, the $1$-WL test is still an elegant and classical method to tackle the graph isomorphism problem that can efficiently tell apart a large number of non-isomorphic graph structures.
elements.sent-2411-452: Moreover, the $1$-WL test provides a natural benchmark against which the theoretical expressivity of GNNs can be compared.
elements.sent-2411-454: For each graph layer $r \in \{1,...,R\}$ there must exist an injective multiset-function $\bm{\oplus _r$ and an injective function $\psi_r$ such that the updating-procedure for atom features defined in Equations~\ref{eq: mpnn_equations can be written in the form \begin{equation}f_{r}(a) = \psi_r(f_{r-1}(a), \oplus_r f_{r-1}(N(a))_{mul}).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-455: This condition guarantees that distinct atom-neighbourhoods are always mapped to distinct atom feature vector updates.
elements.sent-2411-456: The multiset function $\tilde{\bm{\oplus _R$ used in the pooling step~\ref{eq: mpnn_pooling at the $R$-th (i.e.~last) graph layer must be injective and the final pooling function $q$ must be injective in the argument corresponding to the $R$-th graph layer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-457: This condition guarantees that graphs with distinct final multisets of updated atom feature vectors are always mapped to distinct graph-level vectorial representations.
elements.sent-2411-458: Let $\mathcal{G = (A,B)$ be a (finite) graph with a node featurisation function $f : A \to \mathbb{R ^k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-459: We will prove that for each graph layer $r$ there exists an injective map $\alpha_r$ such that \begin{equation}f_r(a) = \alpha_{r}(c_r(a)) \quad \forall a \in A.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-460: This means that the colour $c_r(a)$ of a node $a \in A$ at step $r$ (as computed by the WL algorithm) uniquely determines the node feature vector $f_r(a)$ at step $r$ (as computed by the GNN).
elements.sent-2411-461: Furthermore, the injectivity condition on $\alpha_r$ guarantees that nodes with distinct colours have distinct feature vectors.
elements.sent-2411-462: We show the existence of $\alpha_r$ via induction over $r$.
elements.sent-2411-463: For $r = 0$, it holds for all $a \in A$ that $f_0(a) = \alpha_0(a)$ so we can simply choose the identity map for $\alpha_0$.
elements.sent-2411-464: If we now assume for the induction step that $\alpha_{r-1}$ exists, then we can write \begin{equation}f_r(a) = \psi_r(f_{r-1}(a), \oplus_r f_{r-1}(N(a))_{mul}) = \psi_r(\alpha_{r-1}(c_{r-1}(a)), \oplus_r \alpha_{r-1}(c_{r-1}(N(a))_{mul})_{mul}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-465: Since $\psi_r$, $\bm{\oplus _r$ and $\alpha_{r-1}$ are injective and the composition of injective functions is again injective, it follows that there must be an injective function $\beta_r$ such that \begin{equation}f_r(a) = \beta_r(c_{r-1}(a), c_{r-1}(N(a))_{mul}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-466: We now set $\alpha_r \coloneqq \beta_r \circ h^{-1}$ whereby $h^{-1}$ is the inverse of the hash function used in the $1$-WL algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-467: Once again, $\alpha_r$ is injective since $\beta_r$ and $h^{-1}$ are injective.
elements.sent-2411-468: Moreover, it now holds for all $a \in A$ that \begin{equation}\alpha_r(c_r(a)) = \beta_r (h^{-1}( c_r(a))) = \beta_r(c_{r-1}(a), c_{r-1}(N(a))_{mul}) = f_r(a)\end{equation} which concludes the proof that $\alpha_r$ exists and has the desired properties.
elements.sent-2411-469: Now let $\mathcal{G = (A,B)$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{G = (\tilde{A , \tilde{B )$ be two graphs that can be distinguished with the $1$-WL test.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-470: We want to show that $\mathcal{G}$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{G }$ are then mapped to different graph-level feature vectors by the message-passing GNN.}
elements.sent-2411-471: The fact that $\mathcal{G}$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{G }$ can be distinguished by the $1$-WL test means that at some graph layer $R$ their respective multisets of node colours must be different: \begin{equation}c_R(A)_{mul} \neq \tilde{c}_R(\tilde{A})_{mul} .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-472: Due to the injectivity of $\alpha_R$ this means that the multisets of updated atom feature vectors at the $R$-th layer must also be different: \begin{equation}f_R(A)_{mul} = \alpha_R(c_R(A)_{mul} )_{mul} \neq \alpha_R(\tilde{c}_R(A)_{mul})_{mul} = f_R(\tilde{A}_{mul} ).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-473: Since the multiset-function $\tilde{\bm{\oplus _R$ that is used in the pooling step is assumed to be injective and since the final pooling function $q$ is assumed to be injective in its $R$-th argument as well, we can finally conclude that \begin{equation}R^{l} \ni q(\tilde{\oplus}_0 f_0(A)_{mul},...,\tilde{\oplus}_R f_R(A)_{mul}) \neq q(\tilde{\oplus}_0 f_0(\tilde{A})_{mul},...,\tilde{\oplus}_R f_R(\tilde{A})_{mul}) \in R^{l}\end{equation} which proves that the final vectorial GNN embeddings for $\mathcal{G}$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{G }$ must be different.}}}
elements.formula-2411-053: \begin{equation}R^{l} \ni q(\tilde{\oplus}_0 f_0(A)_{mul},...,\tilde{\oplus}_R f_R(A)_{mul}) \neq q(\tilde{\oplus}_0 f_0(\tilde{A})_{mul},...,\tilde{\oplus}_R f_R(\tilde{A})_{mul}) \in R^{l}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-474: We have shown that all graphs that can be distinguished by the $1$-WL test can also be distinguished by a message-passing GNN under suitable injectivity conditions which means that such GNNs are at least as powerful as the $1$-WL test.
elements.sent-2411-475: For the full proof, including the converse that all graphs that can be distinguished by a message-passing GNN can also be distinguished by the $1$-WL test, we refer the reader to the original article of~\citet{xu2018powerful .}
elements.sent-2411-476: For simplicity we have omitted bond feature vectors in Description~\ref{descrip: 1-WL test and Theorem~\ref{theorem: gnn_expressivity_1-WL , but it is easy to formulate and prove equivalent versions of both statements that take these into account.}}
elements.sent-2411-477: Unlike GCNs, GINs fulfill the injectivity conditions from Theorem~\ref{theorem: gnn_expressivity_1-WL and are thus in the class of maximally expressive message-passing GNNs that are as powerful as the $1$-WL test.}
elements.sent-2411-478: We hypothesise that the increased expressivitiy of GINs compared to theoretically weaker models such as GCNs most strongly translates into superior performance when training data is abundant.
elements.sent-2411-479: This idea is greatly supported by recent experiments in the realm of self-supervised learning where GINs consistently beat GCNs by a large margin when fine-tuned on supervised tasks after pre-training on millions of unlabelled molecular graphs~\citep{hu2019strategies, wang2021molclr .}
elements.sent-2411-480: Arguably the most important contribution of \citet{xu2018powerful was not the invention of the GIN, but the formulation of a mathematical framework to analyse the expressivity of GNNs that was based on well-known concepts from classical graph theory such as the graph isomorphism problem and the $1$-WL test.}
elements.sent-2411-481: Notably, this breakthrough was achieved in parallel with \citet{morris2019weisfeiler who published similar insights and proposed $k$-GNNs as a generalisation of traditional message-passing GNNs.}
elements.sent-2411-482: The $k$-GNN architecture was designed to break through the expressivity barrier posed by the $1$-WL test and move up the WL hierarchy.
elements.sent-2411-483: The $k$-WL tests represent a family of polynomial-time graph isomorphism tests of strictly increasing expressivity (with the exception of the $2$-WL test that is as powerful as the $1$-WL test).
elements.sent-2411-484: Each $k$-WL test represents an extension of the $1$-WL test from Description~\ref{descrip: 1-WL test that operates on $k$-tuples of nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-485: This makes $k$-GNNs prohibitively computationally expensive and normally impossible to use in practice.
elements.sent-2411-486: A potential way forward was recently proposed by~\citet{bouritsas2022improving in the form of graph substructure networks (GSNs).}
elements.sent-2411-487: GSNs are associated with the emerging field of geometric deep learning~\citep{bronstein2017geometric,bronstein2021geometric whose aim it is to effectively generalise deep learning architectures to non-Euclidean domains such as graph structures.}
elements.sent-2411-488: GSNs were designed with the goal of being simultaneously practically applicable and more expressive than the $1$-WL test.
elements.sent-2411-489: The key idea of GSNs is to compute messages in a manner that depends on the structural relationships between the considered nodes.
elements.sent-2411-490: This can be achieved by adding precomputed atomic structural descriptors for both nodes to the message-passing function $(w_r)_{r = 1 ^{R}$ in the procedure outlined in Equations~\ref{eq: mpnn_equations .}}
elements.sent-2411-491: Each node descriptor counts how often the node appears in a particular topological role in all subgraphs of the input graph that are isomorphic to a particular predefined (small) graph.
elements.sent-2411-492: The node descriptors do not get updated and remain static across all graph layers.
elements.sent-2411-493: The a priori choice of basic subgraphs to look out for in the input graphs allows for the establishment of a useful inductive bias tailored to the prediction task at hand: for example, triangles might be relevant in social networks while ring-structures might play an important role in molecular graphs.
elements.sent-2411-494: Since GSNs only represent a slight generalisation of the standard local message-passing framework, their application is computationally feasible; and since they still allow for the inclusion of higher-order structural information in each message-passing step, they can be made strictly more powerful than the $1$-WL test (which then for example enables them to distinguish the graphs from Figure~\ref{fig:wlcounterexample ).}
elements.sent-2411-495: The expressivity of GSNs cannot be easily formulated though within the larger WL hierarchy since suitably designed GSNs can distinguish (for example) some graph pairs in the $3$-WL or $4$-WL class but not all.
elements.sent-2411-496: GSNs have already shown encouraging performance on chemical prediction tasks~\citep{bouritsas2022improving .}
elements.sent-2411-497: Below we give a list of advantages $\bm{(+)}$ and disadvantages $\bm{(-)}$ of message-passing GNNs as a molecular featurisation method.
elements.sent-2411-498: Message-passing GNNs are able to learn features directly from highly explicit graph representations of molecules in a differentiable and task-specific manner.
elements.sent-2411-499: In this particular sense we refer to GNNs as trainable.
elements.sent-2411-500: From a mathematical perspective, the training process for GNNs takes the form of a gradient-based continuous optimisation problem.
elements.sent-2411-501: The differentiability of GNNs could potentially allow them to extract substantially larger amounts of valuable chemical information from molecular graphs than non-differentiable featurisation methods such as ECFPs and PDVs.
elements.sent-2411-502: Many message-passing GNNs still produce useful features for downstream prediction tasks even if the fingerprint length is limited to $l \leq 100$.
elements.sent-2411-503: This can be seen in contrast to ECFPs where the minimum fingerprint length to reach an acceptable level of performance is usually considered to be $l \geq 1024$.
elements.sent-2411-504: The relatively low dimensionality of GNN-based feature vectors can decrease the risk of costly downstream-computations and overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-505: Ability to capture intricate patterns due to model complexity.
elements.sent-2411-506: GNN architectures are often complex and involve large numbers of trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-507: This may enable them to detect nuanced and intricate patterns, especially when the training set is large.
elements.sent-2411-509: The recent development of the GSN~\citep{bouritsas2022improving as discussed in Section~\ref{subsec: gnn_expressivity has shown that GNN models can be made both practical and substantially more powerful than the $1$-WL test (and thus also more powerful than ECFPs) at distinguishing graph structures.}}
elements.sent-2411-511: As discussed in Section~\ref{subsec: gnn_expressivity , many common GNN models such as GCNs are substantially less powerful than the $1$-WL test at distinguishing graph structures and in some cases severely underfit the training set.}
elements.sent-2411-512: Risk of overfitting due to model complexity.
elements.sent-2411-513: The complexity of GNN architectures may lead them to require relatively large amounts of data and/or careful regularisation in order to avoid overfitting and generalise effectively.
elements.sent-2411-514: The success of modern deep-learning architectures lies to a large part in their ability to automatically extract abstract high-level features directly from raw input data.
elements.sent-2411-515: This ability is crucially reliant on the sufficient depth of the neural model since learned features tend to become more abstract and task-specific as they are moving through consecutive network layers.
elements.sent-2411-516: GNNs cannot yet leverage the power of deep architectures as the predictive utility of learned node embeddings tends to decrease sharply after a few GNN layers.
elements.sent-2411-517: The exact reasons and possible solutions for this pathology are still under research~\citep{liu2020towards,jin2022feature,zhang2021evaluating,godwin2021simple, chen2020measuring .}
elements.sent-2411-518: A likely cause might lie in the phenomenon of oversmoothing which refers to a tendency of successively updated node features to eventually become indistinguishable.
elements.sent-2411-520: The graph-level featurisation generated by the global pooling step of a GNN usually consists of a vector of obscure real numbers that cannot be directly interpreted by human experts in any straightforward manner.
elements.sent-2411-521: No chemically reasonable initial embedding of chemical space.
elements.sent-2411-522: The molecular featurisations extracted by untrained GNNs essentially represent extremely noisy random projections of the information contained in the initial molecular graph features.
elements.sent-2411-523: GNNs therefore need to relearn a reasonable embedding from chemical space from scratch every time they are trained on a new problem (unless they have been combined with a suitable self-supervised or transfer learning approach).
elements.sent-2411-524: In particular, this might force GNNs to continuously relearn basic chemical features that are useful across a wide set of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-525: This can be seen in contrast to classical methods like ECFPs and PDVs which automatically generate embeddings of chemical space that have a basic utility for many applications.
elements.sent-2411-527: PDVs and ECFPs can be generated easily via ready-to-use algorithms implemented in widely-used cheminformatics libraries such as the Python-package RDKit~\citep{landrum2006rdkit .}
elements.sent-2411-528: The implementation of a tailored message-passing-GNN model, on the other hand, usually requires familiarity with technically advanced graph-based deep-learning libraries such as PyTorch Geometric~\citep{fey2019fast .}
elements.sent-2411-530: Like most deep-learning models, GNNs involve large numbers of trainable parameters and costly computations related to the multiplication of large matrices.
elements.sent-2411-531: This can make GNN models much slower to train than PDV or ECFP-based models, although this difference in speed can be significantly mitigated if a suitable GPU can be leveraged during GNN training.
elements.sent-2411-532: Just like ECFPs, message-passing GNNs are based on a local neighbourhood-aggregation scheme for the nodes in a graph.
elements.sent-2411-533: By design, they are thus only capable of learning node embeddings that contain features from local circular subgraphs; this prevents information flow between distant nodes during the atom feature updating process.
elements.sent-2411-534: Potential information loss during graph pooling.
elements.sent-2411-535: The global pooling step in a GNN that combines the multisets of updated node embeddings to a final graph-level feature vector can easily turn into a (non-injective) information bottleneck if not designed carefully.
elements.sent-2411-536: Imagine for example two multisets of identical node feature vectors $\{f, f \}_{\text{mul }$ and $\{f, f, f\}_{\text{mul }$; if the popular averaging-operator is used to pool these two multisets, they lead to the same output representation $f$ even though both multisets are different and should thus be mapped to distinct representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-537: A potential way to avoid such pitfalls may be to employ differentiable graph pooling techniques that (at least in theory) can provably approximate any sufficiently regular pooling function~\citep{navarin2019universal .}
elements.sent-2411-538: We have summarised the critical analyses of PDVs~(see Section~\ref{subsec: pdvs_pro_con ), ECFPs~(see Section~\ref{subsec: ecfps_pro_con ) and message-passing GNNs~(see Section~\ref{subsec: gnns_pro_con ) in Table~\ref{tab: mol_feats_pro_cons to provide a final overview before moving on to the systematic computational experiments in Chapter~\ref{chap: qsar_ac_study .}}}}}
